notes.anchor: The consuls have turned out to be just the kind of men I have so often described to you
notes.note: The consuls of 43 BC were Aulus Hirtius and Gaius Vibius Pansa; both died fighting Antony at Mutina in April 43 BC, and Cicero is praising their courage in posthumous retrospect.

"The boy Caesar" (Caesar puer) is Octavian — Gaius Octavius, later Augustus — who was 19 years old and had raised private legions to fight Antony; Cicero's condescending phrase belied his dependence on Octavian's military power.

notes.anchor: the twelfth day before the kalends of May
notes.note: Ante diem duodecimum Kalendas Maias = 20 April 43 BC, the day news of the battles of Mutina (14 and 21 April) reached Rome and was celebrated as a liberation; the Roman calendar counted inclusively from the named day.

The Capitoline Hill, seat of the great temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, was the symbolic religious heart of Rome; a triumphal procession from the Forum to the Capitol was Rome's highest civic honour.

The Rostra was the speaker's platform in the Roman Forum, decorated with the bronze prows (rostra) of captured Antiate ships (338 BC); it was the premier stage for public oratory and political address.

notes.anchor: the cause of the three brothers is one and the same
notes.note: The "three brothers" are Marcus Antonius (Mark Antony), Lucius Antonius, and Gaius Antonius; Cicero argues that Brutus's leniency toward Gaius (his prisoner) is inconsistent with prosecuting the other two as enemies of the state.
